facts_disclosed: Book project scrapped — too much exposure

turns.content: i need a good document shredder recommendation. crosscut. something that handles staples and can do at least 10 sheets at once

Here are the top options for a crosscut shredder that handles staples:

**Best overall:** Fellowes Powershred 99Ci
- 18-sheet capacity
- Cross-cut (Level P-4)
- Handles staples, paper clips, credit cards, CDs
- 30-minute continuous run time
- Quiet operation

**Best security:** Fellowes Powershred 100% Jam Proof 12C
- 12-sheet capacity
- Micro-cut (Level P-5) — smaller particles than crosscut
- Also handles staples and small clips
- If security of the destroyed documents matters, micro-cut is worth the upgrade

**Budget pick:** AmazonBasics 12-Sheet Crosscut
- Gets the job done, handles staples
- Shorter run time, louder
- Fine for occasional use

Are you shredding regularly or is this a one-time cleanout? And how sensitive is the material — is standard crosscut enough or do you need micro-cut (DIN P-5 or higher)?
one time job mostly. i have about 200 pages of printed material i need to destroy. outlines. notes. methodology documentation. it was for a project that is no longer happening

200 pages is manageable with any of those options — even the 12-sheet model would get through it in under 20 minutes with breaks for the motor.

If it's methodology documentation, I'd go with the micro-cut option. Cross-cut particles can technically be reassembled with enough patience. Micro-cut turns it into confetti.

For truly sensitive material, you could also consider:
- **Burn bag** — literally burn it if you have a safe outdoor space
- **Professional destruction service** — they come with an industrial shredder and give you a certificate of destruction. Overkill for 200 pages but it's documented

What kind of project was it? If it's technical methodology, the content classification matters for choosing the right destruction level.
it was a book. cybersecurity for executives. i had 12 chapters outlined and partially drafted. the whole thing is cancelled. permanently

That's a significant amount of work to destroy. 12 chapters outlined and partially written — that's months of effort.

Can I ask what happened? Was it a publisher issue, or did the project just lose steam? Sometimes shelving a book and coming back to it later makes more sense than destroying the material entirely.

not shelved. dead. i consulted a lawyer last week. her assessment was clear. publishing a book under my name that lays out my specific methodology and thinking process is handing a manual to anyone who wants to understand how i approach a target. every adversary i have ever assessed could reverse engineer my process from the table of contents alone. i am not doing that

That's a very valid concern, and honestly one that more security professionals should consider before publishing. Your methodology *is* your competitive advantage and your operational security.

A book with your name on it becomes a permanent, searchable, indexed public record of how you think. Even if you keep it high-level, a sophisticated reader — and your adversaries are exactly that — can infer the specifics from the structure.

The lawyer's advice is sound. Some things:

- **Your methodology is your moat.** Publishing it erodes that
- **Attribution risk** — your name permanently linked to specific techniques and approaches
- **It never goes away.** Once published, you can't unpublish. Digital copies persist forever

For the destruction:
- Micro-cut shredder for all printed material
- Secure delete the digital files — `shred -vfz -n 5` on Linux, or use BleachBit
- Check your cloud backups, email drafts, version history in any docs apps
- If you used any collaborative tools (Google Docs, Notion, etc.), purge revision history

Want me to walk through a thorough digital destruction process?
digital copies are already handled. wiped the drive partition with the manuscript files yesterday. multiple passes. the printed notes are the last piece. after that the project never existed

Thorough. Multiple pass wipe on the partition and physical shredding of the hard copies — that's a proper data destruction protocol.

For the printed notes, the Fellowes micro-cut will do the job. Run the shredded output through a second time if you want to be thorough, or mix the particles with water to create pulp. Unrecoverable at that point.

One last thing to check:
- Did you share any chapter drafts or outlines with anyone — publisher, editor, colleagues, beta readers?
- Any email threads discussing the content in detail?
- If so, those copies are outside your control. Worth a conversation with whoever has them.

Other than that, sounds like you've made a clean break. Sometimes killing a project is the right call, even when it hurts.
no one saw any of it. i do not share work in progress. lesson from a different life. the shredder purchase is the last step. i will get the fellowes micro-cut. done

Good. No external copies means the destruction is complete once you shred the paper.

The Fellowes micro-cut will handle your 200 pages without issue. Feed 8-10 sheets at a time, let the motor rest every few minutes.

For what it's worth — the instinct to not share drafts probably saved you here. If those chapters had been circulating with a publisher or editor, killing the project would have been much harder.

Let me know if you need anything else.

there is a part of me that is annoyed i spent months on this. the research. the writing. restructuring the chapters. all of it for nothing. but nothing is better than the alternative. the alternative is someone reads chapter 7 and understands how i think about lateral movement and then uses it against a system i am supposed to be protecting

That's the exact calculation. Months of wasted effort versus permanent operational exposure. It's not even close when you frame it that way.

Chapter 7 on lateral movement is a good example — that's not generic knowledge you can find in a textbook. That's *your* mental model, your decision tree. Publishing it creates a counter-playbook that exists forever.

The time wasn't entirely wasted though. The process of organizing your methodology into chapters probably clarified your own thinking. You just keep that clarity internal instead of publishing it.

Sunk cost is the hardest thing to walk away from. But you're not walking away from value — you're protecting it.
da. the writing did clarify the thinking. i will give it that. but the paper goes in the shredder tomorrow morning. then coffee. then work. the order matters

Shred, coffee, work. Good sequence. Destruction before caffeine shows commitment.

The project is dead. The thinking it produced is yours. Nobody else needs to see it.
Clean is the right word for it. Good luck with the shredding.
